title: EdgeRelations
lean_explanation:
This row formalises three predicates from the LN's edge-relations block: adjacency between two vertices, "edge into a vertex", and "edge out of a vertex". All three live inside `namespace CDMG`, so each attaches to a `CDMG Node` value `G` via dot notation. The shared binder `Node : Type*` is the ambient vertex carrier ŌĆö matching the LN's silent assumption that vertices live in some fixed type ŌĆö and `[DecidableEq Node]` is inherited from the surrounding `variable` line because the underlying `Finset` membership tests need to decide pair equality in the kernel.

`adjacent (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop` is a one-line renaming of `G.sus v1 v2`, the "some edge of any orientation" predicate from the previous row's notation block. Under the hood, `sus` unfolds to the three-disjunct $(v_1, v_2) \in E \lor (v_2, v_1) \in E \lor (v_1, v_2) \in L$ ŌĆö exactly the LN's stated equivalence in item~i. Because `sus` is symmetric in its arguments (via `CDMG`'s `hL_symm` field for the bidirected disjunct), `adjacent` inherits that symmetry for free.

`into (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) (e : Node ├Ś Node) : Prop` takes a single bound vertex `v` and an ordered pair `e`, and unfolds to the disjunction $(e \in E \land e_2 = v) \lor (e \in L \land (e_1 = v \lor e_2 = v))$. The two clauses correspond to the LN's two edge channels: a directed `E`-edge is "into `v`" only when `v` sits at the head (second coordinate of the pair), while a bidirected `L`-edge is "into `v`" at either endpoint, mirroring the symmetry of $L$ baked into the `CDMG` structure. The requirement that `e` itself live in $E \cup L$ is built into the predicate body ŌĆö no precondition on `e` is threaded through the signature, since an ordered pair outside both fields falls through both disjuncts and makes the predicate vacuously `False`.

`outOf (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) (e : Node ├Ś Node) : Prop` is the directed-only dual: it unfolds to $e \in E \land e_1 = v$, i.e. `v` must sit at the tail of a directed edge. There is no `L`-clause ŌĆö the LN's item~iii enumerates only directed-edge writings, and the rewrite's reconciliation block makes explicit that no $L$-edge is ever out of any vertex.

The shared signature `(v : Node) (e : Node ├Ś Node)` makes `into v` and `outOf v` partially-applied predicates *on edges*, the natural shape for downstream consumers such as the parents/children/siblings sets of the next definition and the edge-removal step under hard interventions.
design_choices:
**Adjacency is the named relation `G.sus`, not the three-disjunct spelled out.** The LN rewrite identifies adjacency with $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$, and the `sus` notation introduced in the previous row already unfolds to $(v_1, v_2) \in E \lor (v_2, v_1) \in E \lor (v_1, v_2) \in L$. Re-typing the body here would duplicate `sus`'s implementation and saddle every downstream proof that case-analyses on adjacency with two parallel names for the same predicate. As a bonus, `adjacent` inherits `sus`'s symmetry for free, which is precisely why the LN chose `sus` (rather than `suh` or `hus`) for adjacency in the first place.

**Both `into` and `outOf` are predicates on *ordered pairs*, not on pairs of vertices.** The LN source block writes "$v_1 \hut v_2$" or "$v_1 \huh v_2$" ŌĆö these are *writings*, not edges ŌĆö and the literal text lists "into $v_1$" and "into $v_2$" as separate sub-clauses while only defining "out of $v_1$". Parameterising over `(v : Node) (e : Node ├Ś Node)` collapses both asymmetries into a single uniformly-quantified predicate: any vertex may be supplied as `v`, and the underlying edge is identified with its ordered pair regardless of how it is textually written. The reconciliation block in the rewritten tex spells out the correspondence with each source writing.

**The vertex `v` and the pair `e` range over the ambient types, with no preconditions on the signature.** `v` is typed `Node`, not `v Ōłł G.V` or `v Ōłł G.J Ōł¬ G.V`, and `e` carries no `e Ōłł E Ōł¬ L` hypothesis. The `CDMG` subset constraints `hE_subset` and `hL_subset` already guarantee that any pair in $E \cup L$ has both coordinates inside the graph, so a stranger `v` outside `G` (or a pair outside $E \cup L$) makes the predicate vacuously `False`. Threading either restriction through the type would force every downstream call site to carry a membership proof it can otherwise discharge from the predicate body itself.

**`into` keeps the `E`- and `L`-clauses separate; `outOf` excludes `L` entirely.** A directed edge contributes "into `v`" only at its head, but a bidirected edge contributes at *both* endpoints because of `hL_symm`. Merging into a unified condition like "$v \in \{e_1, e_2\} \land e \in E \cup L$" would over-include directed edges with `v` at the *tail* ŌĆö which the LN classifies under `outOf`, not `into`. Symmetrically, no $L$-edge is ever out of any vertex, and adding an `L`-clause to `outOf` would deviate from the LN. **Consequence:** "into $v$" and "out of $v$" are *not* complementary on edges incident at $v$; any downstream notion built on `outOf` (out-degree, children, removed in-edges under hard intervention) must treat bidirected edges as contributing *nothing*, not as the remainder after removing in-edges.

**`def`, not `abbrev`.** All three are named LN relations used as abstractions downstream ŌĆö `adjacent` underpins the definition of walks, while `into` and `outOf` underpin parents/children/siblings sets and the edge-removal step under hard interventions. An `abbrev` would auto-unfold to its body at every elaboration site, eliminating the named abstraction; downstream proofs that want the disjunction explicit can `unfold` it manually in one controlled step.
